page_contents_html:
Abnormal liver function tests (LFTs) in adults: practical primary care assessment and referral

Use a pattern-based approach: identify urgent red flags first, classify the dominant abnormality, investigate persistent abnormalities systematically, and stage fibrosis risk in likely chronic liver disease.

  • Immediate escalation: synthetic failure/decompensation signs, severe cholestatic concern, or rapidly worsening derangement.
  • Pattern-led management: hepatocellular, cholestatic, isolated GGT, isolated bilirubin, and synthetic-marker abnormalities are handled differently.
  • Primary care objective: identify treatable causes early and avoid both delayed referral in high-risk cases and unnecessary referral in low-risk stable cases.

accordion_sections.heading: Assessment
accordion_sections.html:
Assess abnormal LFTs using urgency first, then biochemical pattern and trend.

  • Red flags: jaundice with synthetic dysfunction, ascites, encephalopathy, bleeding, marked derangement, or concerning weight loss/cholestasis.
  • Classify dominant pattern: hepatocellular (ALT/AST), cholestatic (ALP/GGT), isolated bilirubin, mixed.
  • Take focused history: alcohol, medicines/supplements, viral risks, metabolic syndrome, autoimmune and family history.
  • Examine for chronic liver disease signs and hepatosplenomegaly; document BMI and cardiometabolic risk.
  • Perform first-line liver screen and ultrasound/fibrosis pathway where indicated.

accordion_sections.heading: Primary care management
accordion_sections.html:
The default approach is pattern-based management with explicit reassessment and escalation rules.

  • Hepatocellular pattern (ALT/AST): review alcohol/medicines, assess metabolic and viral risk, investigate persistent/rising abnormalities, and refer earlier if marked or progressive.
  • Cholestatic pattern (ALP/GGT): confirm hepatic origin, assess for obstruction/cholestatic symptoms, image where indicated, and exclude bone causes when ALP is isolated.
  • Isolated GGT: focus on alcohol/metabolic and medicine review; trend over time rather than reflex referral if otherwise low-risk.
  • Isolated bilirubin: consider benign unconjugated patterns (for example Gilbert context) versus haemolysis/cholestatic disease when atypical.
  • Synthetic markers abnormal: treat as high risk and escalate promptly.
  • Follow-up: repeat low-risk abnormalities after reversible-cause intervention; escalate for worsening trend, persistent unexplained abnormality, or new symptoms.

accordion_sections.heading: Who to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Urgent same-day referral

  • Jaundice with synthetic dysfunction (raised INR/PT or low albumin), ascites, encephalopathy, active bleeding, or severe systemic deterioration.
  • Marked cholestatic picture with concern for obstruction or malignancy.
  • Rapidly worsening LFT profile or severe derangement with clinical concern.

Routine referral

  • Persistent unexplained abnormal LFTs despite structured primary care work-up.
  • Evidence suggesting significant fibrosis/chronic liver disease requiring specialist staging.
  • Diagnostic uncertainty after initial pattern-led investigation.

Include in referral

  • LFT trend and synthetic markers over time.
  • Pattern classification, liver screen and ultrasound/fibrosis results.
  • Alcohol/medication history and interventions already undertaken.

accordion_sections.heading: Who not to refer
accordion_sections.html:
Do not routinely refer stable asymptomatic patients with low-risk mild abnormalities where a likely reversible cause is identified, no red flags are present, and a documented review plan is in place.

Continue primary care management with lifestyle and medication optimisation, repeat testing, and timely escalation if abnormalities persist or worsen.
